analogy: Think of "Culture and Socialisation" through the chapter's own opening images: 63 CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION I INTRODUCTION ‘Culture’, like ‘society’, is a term used frequently and sometimes vaguely. This chapter is meant to help us define it more precisely and to appreciate its different aspects. In everyday conversation, culture is confined to the arts, or alludes... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 63 CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION, INTRODUCTION, CHAPTER 4, CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Culture and Socialisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 63 CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION I INTRODUCTION ‘Culture’, like ‘society’, is a term used frequently and sometimes vaguely.

The text explains that this chapter is meant to help us define it more precisely and to appreciate its different aspects.

The text explains that in everyday conversation, culture is confined to the arts, or alludes to the way of life of certain classes or even countries.

The text explains that sociologists and anthropologists study the social contexts within which culture exists.

The text explains that they take culture apart to try and understand the relations between its various aspects.

The text explains that just like you need a map to navigate over unknown space or territory, you need culture to conduct or behave yourself in society.

The text explains that culture is the common understanding, which is learnt and developed through social interaction with others in society.

The text explains that a common understanding within a group demarcates it from others and gives it an identity.

The text explains that elements are constantly being added, deleted, expanded, shrunk and rearranged.

The text explains that this makes cultures dynamic as functioning units.

The text explains that the capacity of individuals to develop a common understanding with others and to draw the same meanings from signs and symbols is what distinguishes humans from other animals.

The text explains that creating meaning is a social virtue as we learn it in the company of CHAPTER 4 CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION Activity 1 How do you greet another person in your ‘culture’?

The text explains that do you greet different kinds of persons (friends, older relatives, the other gender, people from other groups) differently?

The text explains that discuss any awkward experience you may have had when you did not know how you should greet a person.

The text explains that is that because you did not share a common ‘culture’?

The text explains that but next time round you will know what to do.

The text explains that much of this knowledge is systematically described and conveyed either orally or through books.

The text explains that the This learning prepares us for carrying out our roles and responsibilities in society.

The text explains that we shall discuss this in greater detail later in this chapter.

The text explains that commuter asks autodriver: “Indiranagar?” The verb that conveys the question — “Bartheera?” or “Will you come?” — is implied in the arch of the eyebrow.

The text explains that driver jerks his head in the direction of the back seat if the answer is “Yes”.

The text explains that by contrast, the sociologist looks at culture not as something that distinguishes individuals, but as a way of life in which all members of society habits acquired by man as a member of society” (Tylor 1871).

The text explains that discuss how the visual captures a way of life Two generations later, the founder of the “functional school” of anthro- pology, Bronislaw Malinowski of Poland (1884-1942) wrote: “Culture comprises inherited artifacts, goods, technical process, ideas, habits and values” (Malinowski 1931:621-46).

The text explains that likewise Leslie White had placed a comparable emphasis on culture as a means of adding meaning to objective reality, using the example of people regarding water from a particular source as holy. participate.

The text explains that malinowski happened to be stranded on an island in the Western Pacific during the First World War, and discovered thereby the value of remaining for an extended period with the society one was studying.

The text explains that this led to the establishment of the tradition of “field work” you will read about it in Chapter 5.

The text explains that the last two phrases are the first to refer to culture as a means of directing behaviour.

The text explains that it is true that our developing understanding of social process would draw upon all these areas.

The text explains that in his influential book, Orality and Literacy Walter Ong cites a study of 1971 that states that only 78 of the approximately 3,000 Dimensions of Culture Three dimensions of culture have been distinguished : (i) Cognitive: This refers to how we learn to process what we hear or see, so as to give it meaning (identifying the ring of a cell-phone as ours, recognising the cartoon of a politician).

The text explains that we have already discussed social control in Chapter 2 .

The text explains that a law is a formal sanction defined by government as a rule or principle that its citizens must follow.

The text explains that dominant sections of society apply dominant norms.

The text explains that for example norms that did not allow dalits from drinking water from the same vessel or even source.

The text explains that in urban areas the widespread use of mobile phones, music systems, cars and buses, ATMs (automated teller machines), refri- gerators and computers in everyday life indicates the dependence on technology.

The text explains that every person in modern society plays multiple roles.

The text explains that we have seen in Chapter 1 and in Chapter 3 (particularly in the discussion on religion) how sociology is an empirical and not a normative discipline.

The text explains that ethnocentrism It is only when cultures come into contact with one another that the question of ethnocentrism arises.

The text explains that a modern society is appreciative of cultural difference and does not close its doors to cultural influences from abroad.

The text explains that on the other hand external intervention in the form of conquest or colonisation can also affect deep seated changes in the cultural practices and behaviour of a society.

The text explains that cultural change can occur through changes in the natural environment, contact with other cultures or processes of adaptation.

The text explains that those whose jobs are outsourced to India are said to have been ‘Bangalored’.

The text explains that tribal communities in North East India as well as in middle India have been the worst affected by the loss of forest resources.

The text explains that when a culture is transformed rapidly and its values and meaning systems undergo a radical change then revolutionary change takes place.

The text explains that the French Revolution (1789) transformed French society by destroying the estate system of ranking, abolishing the monarchy, and inculcating the values of liberty, equality and fraternity among its citizens.

The text explains that to return to the point we started with in Chapter 1 about the interplay between the individual and society, we now move on to the concept of socialisation.

The text explains that at the time of birth, the human infant knows nothing about what we call society or social behaviour.

The text explains that socialisation can be defined as the process whereby the helpless infant gradually becomes a self-aware, knowledgeable person, skilled in the ways of the culture into which s/he is born.

The text explains that the International Encyclopedia of Social Science.

The text explains that invitation to Sociology : A Humanistic Perspective.

The text explains that indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU), Unit 9, Agencies of Socialisation.

The text explains that indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU), Unit 8, Nature of Socialisation.

The text explains that anthropology : The Exploration of Human Diversity.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 4: Culture and Socialisation

prelims_traps: Do not treat 63 CULTURE AND SOCIALISATION and INTRODUCTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Culture and Socialisation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
